title: Transboundary LoRa coverage with regulatory enforcement on the Botswana side
summary: A LoRa gateway proposed near Pandamatenga sits ~6 km from the Botswana / Zimbabwe border. The deployment AOI deliberately straddles both countries to study cross-border camera-trap coverage. This scenario attaches an inline `regulatory_profile` for Botswana and runs with `enforce_regulatory: true` so any band/EIRP overrun fails the run before computation. The gateway is configured at the regulator's 868 MHz EIRP cap; the inline profile demonstrates how operators encode the rules they care about without depending on a bundled regulatory catalog (the system ships none — see §3.7).
demonstrates: regulatory_profile catalog entity (§3.7) with inline declaration

regulatory_profile_ref.name: BW-ICASA-style-868-2026

bands.name: Sub-GHz ISM 868
bands.notes: Operator-curated representation; verify against current BOCRA radio frequency plan before deployment.

regulatory_profile_ref.notes: ILLUSTRATIVE values for the seed scenario — DO NOT treat as authoritative regulatory advice. Spec §3.7 disclaims that regulatory_profile is operator-curated and advisory.

notes: Effective EIRP = `tx_power_dbm + tx_antenna.gain_dbi - tx_cable_loss_db`. The seed `lora-gateway-868-omni-6dbi` equipment is configured at 14 dBm + 6 dBi − 1 dB ≈ 19 dBm EIRP, which would *exceed* the inline profile's 14 dBm cap and cause `REGULATORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` to FAIL the run. Operators reproducing this scenario locally should either lower the cloned equipment's `tx_power_dbm` to 9 dBm or relax `enforce_regulatory` to demonstrate the advisory-only path. Pointing this at the failure path is intentional — the scenario doubles as a negative test for the regulatory enforcement code path.
